variants.llm_generated: The Asset Allocation Paradox refers to the tension between the theoretical benefits of diversification and the practical realities of investment management. David Swensen, in his lectures, identifies three sources of returns: diversification, market timing, and security selection. However, he largely dismisses timing and selection as reliable strategies, emphasizing diversification as a way to reduce risk rather than increase returns. Interestingly, Yale's endowment has engaged in market timing, such as shorting technology stocks before the dot-com bubble burst and real estate before the downturn. In 1990, Yale established an 'Absolute Return' unit, allocating about 15% of its portfolio—primarily by reducing US equities—to strategies focused on hedging. The rationale for employing specialized managers includes reducing external management fees and leveraging internal expertise, especially for asset classes like private equity that require active management. Despite these sophisticated strategies, Yale's portfolio experienced significant losses during the 2009 financial crisis, highlighting that even diversified portfolios can face substantial volatility.
variants.llm_humanized: So, the Asset Allocation Paradox is basically about how diversification is supposed to lower risk, but in practice, things get a bit messier. Swensen, in his lectures, talks about three ways to get returns: diversification, timing, and picking the right investments. He mostly says timing and selection aren’t really doable, so focus on diversification. But then, Yale actually did some market timing—like shorting tech stocks before the dot-com crash and real estate before that market tanked. They even set up an 'Absolute Return' group in 1990, putting about 15% of their money into these hedging strategies, mostly by selling off US stocks. They hire managers internally to save on fees and because some investments, like private equity, just can’t be done without hands-on management. Still, even with all this, Yale lost a quarter of its portfolio in 2009, which shows that diversification doesn’t always protect you from big swings.
variants.human_polished: I recommend reviewing Swensen’s lecture series, which is quite insightful. He identifies three sources of investment returns: diversification, timing, and selection. Swensen largely dismisses timing and selection as viable strategies, focusing instead on diversification, which he notes reduces risk rather than increases returns. Interestingly, Yale’s endowment did engage in market timing, such as shorting technology stocks before the dot-com bubble and real estate before the downturn. In 1990, Yale created an 'Absolute Return' unit, allocating about 15% of its portfolio—primarily by reducing US equities—to these hedging strategies. Employing specialized managers internally helps Yale reduce external management fees, which can be substantial given the size of their portfolio, and provides the expertise needed for complex asset classes like private equity. However, despite these efforts and diversification, Yale still lost 25% of its portfolio in 2009, illustrating that even well-diversified portfolios can experience significant volatility.
variants.dictated_spoken: Yeah so I’d really suggest checking out Swensen’s lecture series, it’s actually pretty enlightening. He talks about three main ways you get returns: diversification, timing, and selection. But he kind of throws out timing and selection as not really possible, and just focuses on diversification, which he says cuts risk but doesn’t really boost returns. But then, funny enough, Yale actually did some market timing—they shorted dot-coms before the bubble burst and real estate before that market went down. Back in 1990, they started this Absolute Return unit, put like 15 percent of their money into it, mostly by selling US stocks, and it’s all about these hedging moves. As for managers, the fees Wall Street charges are huge when you’re talking billions, so Yale hires people internally to save money and keep things running. Plus, you need experts to figure out what to sell to keep your ratios right, and for stuff like private equity, you just can’t invest without managers. But even with all that, Yale still lost 25 percent in 2009, so for all the talk about diversification lowering volatility, they still had some pretty big swings the last few years.
